chunk_summary: This vignette differentiates picky eating from pediatric feeding disorder using growth, red flags, and functional impact, with management strategies for each condition.
learning_objectives: Identify key differences between picky eating and pediatric feeding disorder.
Understand the role of growth trajectory and red flags in dietary assessment.
Learn responsive feeding strategies for managing picky eating.
Recognize when to escalate care for suspected pediatric feeding disorder.
key_facts: Picky eating is developmentally common if growth is normal and no red flags are present.
Red flags include weight loss, iron deficiency anemia, choking, prolonged meals, and reliance on supplements.
Responsive feeding includes planned meals/snacks, no grazing, and neutral exposure to new foods.
Limit excessive milk or juice intake to prevent appetite suppression.
Suspected pediatric feeding disorder requires multidisciplinary care, including pediatrics, dietitian, OT/SLP, and psychology.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Typical neophobia differs from a pediatric feeding disorder. Use a brief diet quality screen to map variety (food groups accepted) and a targeted history for red flags.
document_key_topics: Picky eating vs. pediatric feeding disorder
